content.problemStatement:
**Short Answer - 2.5 marks**

Sorting the array [6,5,3,1] using bubble sort.

Initial List
[6,5,3,1]
First Pass:
Compare 6 and 5, swap -> [5,6,3,1]
Compare 6 and 3, swap -> [5,3,6,1]
Compare 6 and 1, swap -> [5,3,1,**6**]
Second Pass
Compare 5 and 3, swap -> [3,5,1,**6**]
Compare 5 and 1, swap -> [3,1,**5**,**6**]
Third Pass
Compare 3 and 1, swap -> [**1**,**3**,**5**,**6**]
The list is now sorted.
[1,3,6,5]
Task
Given a program to sort using bubble sort.
Analyze the code for bubble sort and **submit**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the N integers of the sorted array in non‑decreasing order, separated by a single space. Print a newline after each test case.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The array [6,5,3,1] is sorted by bubble sort to [1,3,5,6].
Sorting the reverse ordered array [3,2,1] results in [1,2,3].

solution.approach: For each test case we apply the classic bubble sort algorithm. We repeatedly scan the array, swapping adjacent elements that are out of order. After the i‑th pass, the largest i elements are guaranteed to be at the end, so the inner loop can stop at N‑i‑1. The algorithm runs in O(N^2) time and O(1) extra space, which satisfies the given constraints (N ≤ 1000).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case, where N is the size of the array.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) auxiliary space (sorting is done in‑place).
